coredata.dc:title: Application of green tea residue-derived carbon dots in the detection of Fe3+ ions and imaging of Caco-2 cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: He, Yu
Liu, Suqing
Xie, Feng
Zhou, Yan
Yang, Xiaosheng
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we successfully synthesized green luminescent carbon dots by using green tea residue as the precursor of carbon source. Based on the internal filter effect (IFE), the fluorescence emission intensity of the probe decreased significantly with the increase of Fe3 + concentration. Based on this, a linear relationship between Fe3 + concentration and the fluorescence emission intensity of the probe was established to semi-quantitatively detect Fe3 +. The results showed that the linear range was 1–400 μM, and the detection limit was 3.58 μM. In addition, the probe was further applied to actual samples, and it was found that the recovery rate was between 97.75% and 104.59%, and the relative standard deviation was below 1.81%. These findings underscore the considerable potential of the carbon dots for practical Fe3+ detection applications. In a further application, the fluorescent carbon dots were diluted and directly employed to stain Caco-2 cells. Remarkably, even after 10 and 20-fold dilutions, the Caco-2 cells displayed vivid fluorescence at the green excitation wavelength. This outcome suggests a promising avenue for the application of fluorescent carbon dots in the field of biology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green tea residue
Carbon dots
Fe3+ detection
Cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV absorption, optimal excitation and emission spectra. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra at excitation wavelengths of 350–400 nm.
(a) (b) (d) is the HR-TEM images of CDs at scale bar of 20 nm, 5 nm and 2 nm, respectively. (c) Particle size distribution of CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum depicting the elemental composition of CDs. (b) High-resolution C 1 s spectrum, (c) N 1 s spectrum, and (d) O 1 s spectrum of CDs.
Effects of salt concentration (a), pH (b), and reaction time (c) on the fluorescence intensity of CDs (λ ex=363 nm).
The fluorescence intensity of different metal ions (400 μM) co-incubated with CDs (50 μL).

(a) The effect of different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–1000 µM) on the fluorescence value of CDs (The excitation wavelength is 363 nm, the slit width is 20, and the voltage is 600 V). Inset: The photographs of Fe3+(0–1000 µM) reacting with CDs under 365 nm UV-Light irradiation. (b) The linear relationship between (F 0-F)/F 0 and Fe3+ (1–400 µM).
(a) UV absorption spectra of CDs with varied Fe3+ solution concentrations. (b) The UV-Vis absorbance spectra of Fe3+ (100 µM).
UV absorption spectra of CDs and Fe3+.
(a) and (b) were the bright field and green fluorescence (λ ex (450–490 nm), λ em ＞520 nm)) images of Caco-2 cells incubated with CDs diluted 20 and 10, respectively.
The carbon dots and the documented Fe3 + detection techniques were contrasted.
The results of Fe3+ in water samples (n=3).

head.ce:title: Application of green tea residue-derived carbon dots in the detection of Fe3+ ions and imaging of Caco-2 cells

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Natural Products Research Center of Guizhou Province, Guizhou Science City

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we successfully synthesized green luminescent carbon dots by using green tea residue as the precursor of carbon source. Based on the internal filter effect (IFE), the fluorescence emission intensity of the probe decreased significantly with the increase of Fe3 + concentration. Based on this, a linear relationship between Fe3 + concentration and the fluorescence emission intensity of the probe was established to semi-quantitatively detect Fe3 +. The results showed that the linear range was 1–400 μM, and the detection limit was 3.58 μM. In addition, the probe was further applied to actual samples, and it was found that the recovery rate was between 97.75% and 104.59%, and the relative standard deviation was below 1.81%. These findings underscore the considerable potential of the carbon dots for practical Fe3+ detection applications. In a further application, the fluorescent carbon dots were diluted and directly employed to stain Caco-2 cells. Remarkably, even after 10 and 20-fold dilutions, the Caco-2 cells displayed vivid fluorescence at the green excitation wavelength. This outcome suggests a promising avenue for the application of fluorescent carbon dots in the field of biology.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron ion (Fe3+) plays a pivotal role in numerous physiological activities, including mitochondrial electron transfer, oxidation/ reduction of biological substrates, and oxygen transfer. As an essential trace element in organisms, it holds a decisive function in the respiration and metabolism of ecosystems and biological systems (Guo et al., 2021). Imbalances in Fe3+ ion levels, either deficiency or excess, can lead to disease (Wen et al., 2020). Insufficient iron ions can lead to anemia, while an overload can cause severe biological disorders and physiological damage, affecting organs such as the pancreas, heart, and other functional systems (Kailasa, Ha, Baek, Kim, & Park, 2019). Furthermore, the continuous threat to human health posed by the escalating Fe3+ ion content in various environmental water bodies due to industrial development necessitates real-time monitoring (Wu et al., 2019). Numerous methods including mass spectrography (He et al., 2022), atomic adsorption spectroscopy (AAS) (Niu et al., 2018), electrochemical method (Abbas et al., 2023), and atomic fluorescence spetrometry (AFS) (Li et al., 2020) for the detection of Fe3+. These methods, known for their high sensitivity, have found extensive application in detecting Fe3+ ions across diverse samples (Wu et al., 2019). However, their complexity, high instrument costs, dependence on skilled labor, and the use of health and environmentally harmful organic solvents make them less favorable (Wang et al., 2021). Consequently, there is a compelling need for the development of a simple, cost-effective detection technology. The fluorescence detection method has emerged as a widely adopted approach in ions sensing and biological imaging, owing to its advantages of simplicity, rapid analysis, and real-time detection capabilities.

In contrast to traditional quantum dots, fluorescent carbon nanoparticles exhibit notable characteristics, including straightforward preparation, robust hydrophilicity, stable photoluminescence, and low toxicity. Therefore, they have garnered significant attention in environmental monitoring, biomarkers, biomedicine, and metal ion analysis (Chen et al., 2019). Carbon dots (CDs) were first reported by Xu et al., in 2004 (Zulfajri et al., 2019), and were described as particles smaller than 10 nm composed of sp2 carbon-based amorphous or crystalline core and carboxylated carbon oxide surface (Liu et al., 2017). Leveraging their exceptional optical properties, CDs have found applications in environmental, biomedicine and information (Krishnaiah et al., 2022). Currently, various synthetic routes for CDs preparation, including hydrothermal synthesis, thermal decomposition, microwave methods, and more, have been reported (Irmania et al., 2020). However, a drawback of many of these methods is the utilization of expensive carbon sources (Chen et al., 2019). In response, biological carbon sources have gained extensive attention due to their eco-friendly attributes, species diversity, and ample availability (Xu, Fan, Zhang, Wang, Fu, & She, 2019), and their complex elemental makeup results in high water solubility, outstanding optical characteristics, and hypotoxicity (Krishnaiah et al., 2022). At present, many researchers have successfully synthesized CDs by using various natural substances. Sweet potatoes were utilized by Shen, Shang, Chen, Wang, & Cai to create fluorescent CDs for the imaging of cancer cells and the detection of Fe3 + ions. Fluorescent CDs with anti-cancer and anti-bacterial properties, as well as selective Pb2+ ion detection, were made from mushrooms by Boobalan et al. (2020). Other natural substances such as rose-heart radish (Liu, Diao, Chang, Wang, Li, & Wenlong, 2017), Poa pratensis (Krishnaiah et al., 2022) and Mango peel (Jiao, Li, Qin, Zhang, Huang, & Xu, 2019) have also used in the synthesis of CDs. Although more and more natural materials have been reported to be successfully used in the synthesis of CDs, the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots by these methods often takes a lot of time, and the linear range of Fe3 + detection is not very wide. Shen et al. (2017) et al. synthesized CDs using sweet potatoes as a carbon source. But the synthesis process calls for heating at 180 °C for eighteen hours after three hours of magnetic stirring at 80 °C, not only does the process of creating fluorescent carbon dots require a significant amount of time, but the fluorescence probe can identify Fe3 + within the 1–100 μM range. Jayaweera et al. (2019) synthesized fluorescent carbon dots using durian shell waste as the raw material, however, they require heating at 210 °C for 12 hours, and the detection of Fe3 + has a narrow linear range of 0–20 μM. Thus, it is still an issue worth investigating how to create a green, extremely sensitive fluorescence sensor for cell imaging and heavy metal ion detection.

Tea has always been loved by people in China, and green tea is one of the most popular tea varieties, which rich in tea polyphenols and rich source. Thus, we decided to use green tea residue as the raw material for hydrothermally producing luminous CDs directly. Realizing the waste utilization of tea residue. The synthesis process does not require excessive addition of other compounds, and the methods was more environmentally friendly than previously reported. Subsequently, through characterisation, the CDs' microstructure and elemental makeup were examined. Additionally, we used it to detect Fe3+ ions and then to image Caco-2 cells in cells. To explore its potential applications in ions detection and imaging.

Green tea raw materials were purchased from a local supermarket (Le 'erle supermarket of Guizhou Medical University, Guiyang City, Guizhou Province), After being recovered, the tea leaves were immersed in two batches of boiling water until the water turned nearly clear. The green tea residue utilized in this investigation was then obtained by drying it at 60 °C, crushing it with a domestic grinder, and passing it through a mesh screen (20 mesh). NaOH(Analytical Reagent (AR)), NaCl（Content >99.5%, AR）were allsourced from Kermel Chemical Agent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 0.01 M, pH 7.0) and Polythylene glycol (average Mn 200, AR) was acquired from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HCl (36.0–38.0%, AR) was obtained from Chongqing Chuandong Chemical Co., Ltd. High Glucose Medium, Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM), was supplied from Gibao Co., Ltd. Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) was obtained from vivacell Co., Ltd. Quinine sulfate dihydrate (98%) was provided by Aladdin Industrial Corporation.

Precisely, 0.2 g of tea residue powder was accurately weighed and placed in a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave high-pressure reactor. Subsequently, 10 mL ultrapure water and 5 mL polyethylene glycol were added. After 30 min of shaking, the reaction was conducted at 200 ºC for 5 hours. To get a light brown solution, centrifuge at 4500 rpm for 20 minutes after chilling. The solution was filtered through a 0.22 μM filter membrane to remove large particles. Finally, the solution was transferred to a dialysis bag (1000 MWCO) and dialyzed for 24 hours to provide a CDs solution for further use.

The UV-1700SPC spectrophotometer (Macylab Instruments, China) provided the UV-Vis spectra. To investigate the morphology and lattice features of CDs, Images of high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) were taken using Jem-2100 F (Jeol, Japan). 250 xi spectrometer (Thermo ESCALAB, USA) was utilized to perform XPS on CDs. D8advance (Buker) was used to performed X-ray diffraction (XRD). The fluorescence spectrum was obtained using the F970 Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer from Leng Guang, China.

CDs' quantum yield was determined using a documented method, and computations were carried out by contrasting them with reference substances. In this experiment, quinine sulfate (refractive index η of 1.33, φ=0.54) was chosen as the standard. The quinine sulfate aqueous solution and the CDs were constantly diluted during the measurement to guarantee that the absorbance (at 360 nm) stayed below 0.05. The fluorescence emission wavelength at a specific excitation wavelength (360 nm) was determined, and the integral area of fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded. Subsequently, these absorbance and integrated fluorescence values were utilized to determine the quantum yield through the following procedure:
(1)
φ =
φ
R
×
I
I
R
×
A
R
A
×
η
2
η
R
2

Here, φ represents the quantum yield, I refer to the fluorescence intensity integral under 360 excitation wavelength. A stands for the absorbance, and η signifies the refractive index of 0.1 M H2SO4 and water, with values set at 1.33. The subscript R designates the known standard compound in this context.

Fe3+ ionic solutions of varying concentrations were added to 50 µL CDs solution, and then diluted to 3 mL with ultrapure water to ensure a Fe3+ concentration range of 0–400 µM. Following thorough mixing and a 10 min reaction at room temperature, fluorescence intensity was measured at an excitation wavelength of 363 nm, a bandwidth of 20, and a voltage of 600 V. The feasibility of using the fluorescent probe for Fe3+ analysis and detection was evaluated by quantifying the connection between various Fe3+ concentrations and fluorescence intensity. An ion selectivity experiment was conducted following the same procedure, with 400 µM different ions (Cd2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+) added to a 50 µL CDs solution. After complete mixing, fluorescence measurements were performed to obtain the fluorescence values of different ions. This aims to explore the selectivity of the probe for Fe3 + detection.

The feasibility of the practical application of the fluorescent sensor was investigated by referring to the methodology of Liu, Diao, Cheng, Wang, Li, & Wei, 2017 and Pan et al. (2018)，drinking and lake water samples were collected from Guizhou Medical University. After the water sample was taken back, it was boiled and passed through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to eliminate large particle impurities. The selective determination was carried out according to the above method. The standard solution of Fe 3+ was added to ensure that the final concentration was 20,50,100 μM, and each concentration was paralleled three times to carry out the standard recovery experiment.

In DMEM (10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS)) in an incubator with 5% CO2, Caco-2 human colorectal cancer cells were cultured. Upon reaching the logarithmic growth phase, the cells were trypsinized using trypsin-EDTA digestive solution. Subsequently, the cells were grown at 37 °C after being inoculated in a 96-well plate. Cell growth was monitored under a microscope, and when the cells covered the bottom of the medium, cell labeling experiments were conducted. CDs were prepared by diluting 10 and 20 times with fresh DMEM medium. After washing the cells with PBS (pH 7.0), they were stained with fresh medium containing CDs and incubated at 37 ℃ for 24 h. Rinse three times with PBS buffer after incubation to get rid of any leftover stains. Ultimately, images were examined and collected using the inverted fluorescent microscope.

As a fundamental characteristic of fluorescent nanomaterials, optical properties play a crucial role in establishing the groundwork for their sensing applications. Both an ultraviolet-visible and a fluorescence spectrophotometer were used to examine the fluorescence characteristics of the manufactured CDs. The synthesized CDs' UV absorbance, ideal excitation, and emission wavelengths are shown in Fig. 1(a). Two distinct absorption peaks can be seen in the UV range, as the figure illustrates. The n→π* transition of CO and the π→π * transition of CC are linked to the narrow and intense peak at 203 nm and the comparatively weak absorption peak at 267 nm, respectively. (Tammina et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2021). Simultaneously, the CDs exhibited an optimal excitation wavelength of 363 nm and an optimal emission wavelength around 439 nm. Fig. 1(b) demonstrates the variation in the emission wavelength of the CDs with changing excitation wavelengths. The figure illustrates a close relationship between the emission wavelength and the excitation wavelength when the latter ranges between 350 and 400 nm. This characteristic of CDs has not been confirmed, some studies suggest that this characteristic of CDs was caused by the presence of particles of different sizes on the surface of CDs, some researches also believe that this may be due to the radiation recombination of CDs, but the specific reason remains to be found (Shen et al., 2017; Gunjal et al., 2019). Finally, the quantum yield of CDs was measured to be 5.00% by the standard reference method.

HR-TEM was used to characterize the size distribution and structure of CDs. Fig. 2(a) shows that the CDs are spherical, and there is no aggregation between the particles, and the dispersion is good. In addition, Fig. 2(b) and (d) provide evidence of the internal lattice structure of carbon dots. From Fig. 2(d), it can be seen that the lattice spacing is 0.209 nm, indicating the existence of graphite carbon lattice arrangement (Liu et al., 2017). Fig. 2(c) is the size distribution histogram of CDs. The results show that the size of CDs is between 1.18 and 3.29 nm, and the average particle size is 2.13 nm. The crystallinity of CDs was evaluated using XRD. Fig. 3 shows a distinct and intense diffraction peak appeared around 21.62°, indicating the semicrystalline nature of CDs (Kailasa et al., 2019). Typically, the XRD diffraction peak of graphite carbon is situated at 26.5°. However, in our prepared CDs, the XRD diffraction peak was observed at 21.62°, showing a slight deviation from the conventional peak. This transition can be linked to a decrease in ordered layer sp2 in the carbon structure. (Tammina et al., 2020). Both HR-TEM and XRD techniques corroborate the graphite carbon structure of CDs.

The composition of functional groups in CDs was investigated using FT-IR. Fig. 4 shows a broad distinctive peak at 3401.95 cm−1, which is most likely caused by the tensile vibration of the N-H bonds. (Zhang et al., 2021). The peak at 2869.58 cm−1 indicated the of C-H bonds (Zong et al., 2014). The stretching vibration at 1650.03 cm−1 is attributed to C = O (Atchudan, Atchudan, Perumal, Salama, Lee, & Jeon, 2022). Additionally, several weak peaks at 1246.64, 1291.21 were related with the C-OH, O-H, respectively (Chen et al., 2020). The peak at 1061.37 cm−1 represents the tensile vibration of C-O (Pang et al., 2021). The characteristic peaks at 1349.86 cm−1, and 1453.03 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of C-N (Huang, Zhao, Sun, Li, Zhang, Shi et al., 2024). Other peaks between 829.19 and 934.71 cm−1 and the 521.95 cm−1 have been linked to C-O bending vibrations. (Zulfajri et al., 2019).

XPS was employed to characterize CDs and elucidate their elemental composition further. In ( Fig. 5(a)), it is evident that CDs mainly consist of three elements: C 1 s (285.91 eV), N 1 s (399.47 eV), and O 1 s (532.25 eV), with an atomic ratio of 62.77:2.75:32.48 (Hu et al., 2020). The C 1 s (Fig. 5(b)) predominantly features three binding energies: CC (284.81 eV), C-O (286.28 eV), and CO (287.93 eV) (Jia et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2017). Similarly, the N 1 s (Fig. 5(c)) comprises of four peaks: CN-H (398.73 eV), N-H (399.82 eV), C-N (400.68 eV), and N-H (401.83 eV) (Shangguan et al., 2017; Yan, Liu, Li, Huang, & Zhen, 2019). The O 1 s (Fig. 5(d))exhibits three fitting peaks: CO (531.23 eV), OC-O (532.53 eV), and C-O (533.18 eV) (Yang et al., 2020; Yan, Liu, Li, Huang, & Zhen, 2019). The elemental and group composition of CDs determined by XPS characterisation were identical to the FT-IR results. Both approaches revealed that CDs were mostly made of C, N, and O, as well as the -OH groups.

To evaluate the probe's fluorescence stability, we examined the effect of salt content, pH, and reaction time on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Fig. 6(a) shows that the fluorescence of CDs remains essentially unaltered in the NaCl concentration range of 0–1.0 M. Fig. 6(b) reveals that within the pH range of 3–9, the fluorescence of CDs exhibits minimal variation. However, at pH<3 and pH>9, the fluorescence of CDs was considerably impacted by pH, indicating that alterations in pH might modify the surface functional group structure of CDs, leading to changes in fluorescence intensity (Chen et al., 2020). Under neutral conditions, the fluorescence intensity of CDs is essentially unaffected by pH. Consequently, we conducted the entire experiment under neutral conditions. In Fig. 6(c), the fluorescence quenching process is observed to be completed after a 10-min reaction of reaction between CDs and 50, 160, 200 µM Fe3+. Hence, 10 min was identified as the optimal testing point for the binding of Fe3+ to CDs.

By exploring several common metal ions (Pb2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Co2+ and Fe3+) in water, the possibility of Fe3+ specific detection was evaluated. In comparison to other ions, Fe3+ exhibited a pronounced ability to quench the fluorescence of CDs ( Fig. 7). This quenching effect could be related to Fe3+ stronger attraction for groups like -OH in CDs. As a result, the CDs can serve as a promising fluorescent sensor for detecting of Fe3+ ions.

According to Fig. 8(a), CDs fluorescence intensity fell dramatically as Fe3 + concentration increased. CDs fluorescence drastically decreased and the quenching degree was practically 90% of the concentration Fe3+ was 1000 μM. Fe3 + concentration and CDs fluorescence have a strong linear relationship in the range of 1 ∼ 400 μM (Fig. 8(b)), the linear equation is (F 0-F) / F 0 = 0.0017x + 0.0171(where F 0 and F represent the fluorescence values without Fe3+ and with Fe3+, respectively, and x represents the C(Fe 3+)), yielded a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9924. The detection limit (LOD) was 3.58 µM (S/N=3), which was lower than the hygienic standard for drinking water in China (0.3 mg/L, GB-5749–2006).

In Table 1 compares the probe with the published probe for detecting Fe3 +, the findings demonstrate that the method has a wide linear range and a low detection limit when compared with these methods. This suggests that the fluorescent sensor is more sensitive to Fe3 + ions and can be used as an efficient fluorescent sensor for Fe3 + detection.

Furthermore, we investigated the UV-Vis absorption spectra of CDs solution with varying concentrations of added Fe3+. In Fig. 9(a), CD absorbance peaks in the 190–500 area climbed progressively as Fe3+ level increased. Notably, no new absorption peaks emerged in the spectrum, indicating that the addition of Fe3+ enhanced the absorption of CDs without generating new compounds. As shown in Fig. 9(b) and Fig. 10, Fe3+ ions began to exhibit ultraviolet absorption from 200 nm, with a distinct ultraviolet absorption between 200 and 550 nm, which overlaps with the optimal emission wavelength of CDs (439 nm). This suggests that Fe3+ can absorb the fluorescence emitted by excited CDs, resulting in a reduction in the emission intensity of CDs, which a characteristic manifestation of the internal filtering effect (Gong et al., 2019). Additionally, XPS and FT-IR studies have revealed that the CDs contain a significant amount of -OH, and the interaction between -OH and Fe3+ may cause structure changes in CDs, greatly reducing their fluorescence (Shen et al., 2017).

To assess the practicability of the fluorescence detector, we implemented it for the detection of Fe3+ in real water samples. The analysis involved utilizing standard recovery method, and the results are provided in Table 2. The recovery rates ranged from 98.30% to 104.59% for tap water and from 97.75% to 103.56% for lake water. The relative standard deviation (RSD) values varied from 0.39% to 1.81% for tap water and from 0.37% to 1.27% for lake water. These findings underscore the reliability, feasibility, and sensitivity of the prepared sensor, affirming its suitability for the accurate detection of Fe3+ in real water samples.

As seen in Fig. 11(a) and (b), we employed the probe to conduct cell imaging studies on Caco-2 cells in order to investigate the utilization of the probe in cell imaging. After being diluted 20 and 10 times, respectively, the CDs were incubated with the cells for 24 h. The figure shows that upon co-cultivation of CDs with Caco-2 cells, produce a vivid green light in the green field. Additionally, we observed that the cells had a healthy shape and were growing well, which suggested that the fluorescent probe was lowly hazardous and had good biocompatibility. Furthermore, by looking at the fluorescence images, it was discovered that the cytoplasmic portion also clearly showed green light in the green field, suggesting that the fluorescent CDs were widely dispersed throughout the cells and could be efficiently absorbed by the endocytosis mechanism. These findings suggest that the probe is a perfect reagent for imaging Caco-2 cells.

This research introduces a novel approach to synthesizing carbon dots (CDs) utilizing green tea residue as a precursor, a strategy that not only underscores the sustainable use of biomass waste but also enhances the environmental applicability of nanomaterials. The synthesized CDs exhibit remarkable properties, including excellent water solubility, outstanding biological compatibility, and hypotoxicity, setting a new benchmark in the field of fluorescent nanomaterials. This study demonstrates that, despite CDs' low quantum yield of 5.0%, their strong sensitivity to Fe3 + ions cause a considerable quenching of their emission peak at 439 nm. This selectivity is particularly notable, as it effectively eliminates interference from other metal ions, a common challenge in the detection of specific ions in complex samples. Our findings showcase the CDs’ potent application as a fluorescent sensor for Fe3+, with a detection limit (3.58 µM) that is not only lower than the safe contact value for Fe3+ in China’s drinking water standards but also exhibits superior performance compared to existing probes. Moreover, the practicality of these CDs was validated through the detection of Fe3+ in real water samples, achieving high recovery rates and consistent reliability across different water types. Additionally, the cell labeling assays revealed another dimension of the CDs’ utility. Their low toxicity and excellent solubility rendered them ideal for in vivo imaging materials, particularly demonstrated through the vibrant green fluorescence observed in Caco-2 cells. Our research is opening new avenues for the development of eco-friendly and efficient sensing and imaging technologies.

Suqing Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. yu He: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yan Zhou: Validation, Investigation. Feng Xie: Validation, Investigation. Xiaosheng Yang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present research was financially supported by Guizhou Province Department of Science and Technology (ZK[2022]348, ZK[2024]180) and Doctoral Initiation Fund of Guizhou Medical University (YJ20077).
